symbols: white figure tumbling within a circle — the angel cast down from heavenly order, trapped in descent
blue winged silhouette in the upper ring — the original angelic form being lost or distorted
striped vertical lines — the rigid order of creation from which the angel is expelled
circles as cosmic orbits — the celestial spheres through which the fallen being plummets
description: A surrealist composition set against a background of fine vertical green-and-yellow stripes. Two circular forms dominate the image: a larger yellow-gold circle in the lower center contains a white figure that appears to be tumbling or falling, its limbs splayed. Above and to the right, a smaller circle bordered in red and blue stripes contains a blue silhouette of a winged figure. In the upper left corner, a small white bird or angular shape flies against a pale blue sky above the striped field. The overall effect is geometric and dreamlike, combining flat pattern with symbolic figuration.
theological_description: While this is a surrealist interpretation, the title and imagery evoke the biblical theme of angelic rebellion and expulsion from heaven. Isaiah 14:12 records the fall of the morning star: 'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn!' — traditionally interpreted as a reference to Satan's primordial rebellion. Jesus himself declared, 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven' (Luke 10:18). Revelation 12:7-9 narrates the war in heaven where Michael and his angels cast out the great dragon and his angels, 'and there was no longer any place for them in heaven.' The surrealist treatment — the figure trapped within a geometric circle, plummeting through an ordered cosmos of rigid stripes — captures something of the theological reality: the fallen angel is expelled from the harmony of creation, cast out of the ordered beauty of God's heavenly court. The blue winged silhouette above may represent the original angelic glory now lost, while the tumbling white figure below shows the diminished, disoriented state of the rebel. Pride, the root sin identified in Ezekiel 28:17 ('Your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor'), transforms the highest creature into the most wretched.

scripture_refs.reason: The fall of the Day Star from heaven is the foundational Old Testament text for the imagery of an angel cast down
The war in heaven resulting in the dragon and his angels being thrown down to earth
Jesus's declaration that he saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven
The guardian cherub cast down from the mountain of God because of pride in his beauty
